---
title: "10 · Determinant, Trace, Eigenvalues"
subtitle: "Three numbers that summarise a matrix — and three that don't change under change of basis."
lesson-id: "mml.determinant-trace-eigen"
tier: "mml"
prerequisites: ["mml.projections-rotations", "t5.eigenvalues"]
unlocks: ["mml.cholesky"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Can you compute the determinant of $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$? — see
   [Tier 5 — Matrices](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/01-matrices/index.qmd).
2. Can you state what an eigenvalue/eigenvector is? — see
   [Tier 5 — Eigenvalues](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd).
3. Can you compute the trace of $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$? — same prerequisite.
4. Do you remember the change-of-basis formula $\boldsymbol{A}' = \boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{S}$? — see [MML — 05](../05-rank-and-linear-mappings/index.qmd).

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

Slide a $2 \times 2$ matrix's entries. The widget shows the matrix
applied to a unit-circle worth of vectors. The **shape** of the
resulting ellipse tells you the matrix's geometry:

- **area** of the ellipse = $|\det \boldsymbol{A}|$;
- **sum of axis lengths' squares** of the symmetric part = $\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}^\top \boldsymbol{A})$ — the Frobenius norm squared;
- **principal axes** of the ellipse = eigenvectors of $\boldsymbol{A}^\top \boldsymbol{A}$.

```{ojs}
viewof a11 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 2, step: 0.1, label: "a₁₁:"})
viewof a12 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.1, label: "a₁₂:"})
viewof a21 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 0, step: 0.1, label: "a₂₁:"})
viewof a22 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.1, label: "a₂₂:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const det = a11*a22 - a12*a21;
  const tr  = a11 + a22;
  const ellipse = [];
  for (let t = 0; t <= 2 * Math.PI + 0.01; t += 0.05) {
    const x = Math.cos(t), y = Math.sin(t);
    ellipse.push({x: a11*x + a12*y, y: a21*x + a22*y});
  }
  const Plot = await import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm");
  return html`<div>
    <div style="font:13px sans-serif; margin-bottom:4px;">
      tr(A) = <b>${tr.toFixed(2)}</b>,  det(A) = <b>${det.toFixed(2)}</b>,  area of image = <b>${Math.abs(det).toFixed(2)}π</b>
    </div>
    ${Plot.plot({
      width: 460, height: 360,
      x: {domain: [-5, 5]}, y: {domain: [-5, 5]}, grid: true,
      marks: [
        Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#bbb"}),
        Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#bbb"}),
        Plot.line(ellipse, {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "steelblue", strokeWidth: 2, fill: "rgba(70,130,180,0.15)"})
      ]
    })}
  </div>`;
}
```

Drag to make $\det \boldsymbol{A} = 0$ — the ellipse collapses to a line. Drag to make $\boldsymbol{A}$ a rotation ($a_{11} = a_{22} = \cos\theta$, $a_{12} = -\sin\theta$, $a_{21} = \sin\theta$) — area stays 1.

## Wonder

1. The determinant equals the *area-scaling factor* of the unit square. What if the determinant is negative — what's happening?
2. The trace is the sum of diagonal entries, but also the sum of eigenvalues. Why would those two be equal?
3. If you change basis, all three (det, trace, eigenvalues) stay the same. Could there be more invariants?
4. A matrix with all eigenvalues equal to zero — what does it do geometrically?

## Discover

Six problems building from determinant to eigenpair to invariants.

**D1.** Compute $\det \boldsymbol{A}$ for $\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 \cdot 4 - 1 \cdot 2 = 10$.
</details>

**D2.** For $\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, find eigenvalues by solving $\det(\boldsymbol{A} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I}) = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\det \begin{pmatrix} 2 - \lambda & 1 \\ 0 & 3 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = (2 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) = 0$. Eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 2, \lambda_2 = 3$. For triangular matrices the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries.
</details>

**D3.** Find an eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 2$ in D2.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Solve $(\boldsymbol{A} - 2\boldsymbol{I})\boldsymbol{v} = \boldsymbol{0}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{v} = \boldsymbol{0}$. Second entry zero, first free. $\boldsymbol{v} = (1, 0)$ (and any scalar multiple).
</details>

**D4.** Verify for D2: $\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$ and $\det \boldsymbol{A} = \lambda_1 \lambda_2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Trace: $2 + 3 = 5 = 2 + 3$. ✓ Determinant: $2 \cdot 3 = 6 = 2 \cdot 3$. ✓
This is general: $\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}) = \sum_i \lambda_i$ and $\det \boldsymbol{A} = \prod_i \lambda_i$. (Proof via Vieta's formulas for the characteristic polynomial.)
</details>

**D5.** Show that if $\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n$ are the eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{A}$, then they are also the eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{S}$ for any invertible $\boldsymbol{S}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\det(\boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{S} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I}) = \det(\boldsymbol{S}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{A} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I}) \boldsymbol{S}) = \det \boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \cdot \det(\boldsymbol{A} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I}) \cdot \det \boldsymbol{S} = \det(\boldsymbol{A} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I})$. The characteristic polynomials are equal, so the eigenvalues are equal. **Eigenvalues are invariants of the linear map**, not of the matrix.
</details>

**D6.** For a symmetric matrix $\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{A}^\top$, show that eigenvalues are real and eigenvectors with distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For orthogonality: if $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{v}_1 = \lambda_1 \boldsymbol{v}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{v}_2 = \lambda_2 \boldsymbol{v}_2$, compute $\boldsymbol{v}_1^\top \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{v}_2$ two ways.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{v}_1^\top \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{v}_2 = \lambda_2 \boldsymbol{v}_1^\top \boldsymbol{v}_2$ (using $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{v}_2 = \lambda_2 \boldsymbol{v}_2$).
$\boldsymbol{v}_1^\top \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{v}_2 = (\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{v}_1)^\top \boldsymbol{v}_2 = \lambda_1 \boldsymbol{v}_1^\top \boldsymbol{v}_2$ (using $\boldsymbol{A}^\top = \boldsymbol{A}$).
So $(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) \boldsymbol{v}_1^\top \boldsymbol{v}_2 = 0$. If $\lambda_1 \ne \lambda_2$, the inner product is zero. (Reality of eigenvalues: similar argument with complex conjugates.)
**This is why every covariance matrix (symmetric) has an orthogonal eigenbasis — and that's PCA in one line.**
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The word **eigenvalue** is half-translated from German *Eigenwert* — "own value" — coined by **David Hilbert** circa 1904 in the context of integral operators. The original concept was much older: **Cauchy** in 1829 (vibrations of an elastic body), and **Lagrange** in 1762 (motions of a rotating rigid body) had both worked with "characteristic values" of symmetric matrices. The English-speaking world resisted the German for decades and used "characteristic value" — until quantum mechanics borrowed Hilbert's term wholesale.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**Determinant, trace, and eigenvalues are *intrinsic*** — they survive change of basis. Determinant tells you the volume scaling. Trace adds the eigenvalues. Eigenvalues say "in this direction the matrix acts like a scalar." Together they characterise the matrix up to similarity.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}
The **determinant** of a square matrix $\boldsymbol{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the scalar $\det \boldsymbol{A}$ equal to $|\det \boldsymbol{A}|$ = volume scaling factor of the unit cube under $\boldsymbol{x} \mapsto \boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{x}$, with sign $+1$ for orientation-preserving and $-1$ for orientation-reversing maps.

The **trace** is $\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}) = \sum_i a_{ii}$.

An **eigenvalue / eigenvector pair** $(\lambda, \boldsymbol{v})$ satisfies $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{v} = \lambda \boldsymbol{v}$ with $\boldsymbol{v} \ne \boldsymbol{0}$. The eigenvalues are the roots of the **characteristic polynomial** $p_{\boldsymbol{A}}(\lambda) = \det(\boldsymbol{A} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I})$.

Two foundational identities:
$$
\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}) = \sum_i \lambda_i, \qquad \det \boldsymbol{A} = \prod_i \lambda_i.
$$
All three quantities (det, trace, set of eigenvalues) are **similarity invariants**: unchanged by $\boldsymbol{A} \to \boldsymbol{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{S}$.

::: {.callout-warning}
**Sūtra.** *Determinant = volume; trace = sum of eigenvalues; eigenvalue = "the direction where the matrix is a scalar."*
:::
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
**Watch out:**

1. *"All matrices have $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors."* — Only if the characteristic polynomial has distinct roots (or the matrix is normal — including symmetric). $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ has only one independent eigenvector despite being $2 \times 2$.
2. *"Eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{A}$ and $\boldsymbol{A}^\top$ are different."* — They're identical. $\boldsymbol{A}$ and $\boldsymbol{A}^\top$ have the same characteristic polynomial (det is invariant under transpose).
3. *"Trace and determinant are independent."* — They are linked through the characteristic polynomial. For $2 \times 2$ matrices: $p(\lambda) = \lambda^2 - \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}) \lambda + \det \boldsymbol{A}$.
:::

## Apply

### Worked example

For $\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, find all eigenvalues and eigenvectors.

**Step 1.** Characteristic polynomial:
$\det(\boldsymbol{A} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I}) = (4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - 2 = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = (\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2)$.

**Step 2.** Eigenvalues: $\lambda = 5, 2$. Check: trace $= 4 + 3 = 7 = 5 + 2$. ✓ Det $= 12 - 2 = 10 = 5 \cdot 2$. ✓

**Step 3.** For $\lambda = 5$: $(\boldsymbol{A} - 5\boldsymbol{I}) \boldsymbol{v} = \boldsymbol{0}$ ⇒ $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{v} = \boldsymbol{0}$. First row: $-v_1 + v_2 = 0$, so $\boldsymbol{v}_1 = (1, 1)$.

**Step 4.** For $\lambda = 2$: $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{v} = \boldsymbol{0}$. $2 v_1 + v_2 = 0$, so $\boldsymbol{v}_2 = (1, -2)$.

**Answer:** $\boldsymbol{A}$ has eigenpairs $(5, (1, 1))$ and $(2, (1, -2))$. The matrix stretches along $(1, 1)$ by factor 5 and along $(1, -2)$ by factor 2. The two directions are *not* orthogonal because $\boldsymbol{A}$ is not symmetric.

### Warm

**W1.** What is $\det \boldsymbol{I}_n$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1$. The identity preserves volume and orientation.
</details>

**W2.** Trace and eigenvalues of $\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Trace $= 7$. Eigenvalues $= 5, 3, -1$ (diagonal). $\det = -15$.
</details>

**W3.** What does $\det \boldsymbol{A} = 0$ imply about $\boldsymbol{A}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{A}$ has zero as an eigenvalue, equivalently a non-trivial null space, equivalently is singular (no inverse), equivalently rank $< n$.
</details>

### Working

**Wo1.** Prove $\det(\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{B}) = \det \boldsymbol{A} \cdot \det \boldsymbol{B}$ for square $\boldsymbol{A}, \boldsymbol{B}$. (Just use the volume interpretation.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Composition of linear maps scales volume by the product of the individual scalings. The signed area of $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B}([0,1]^n)$ equals (signed area of $\boldsymbol{B}([0,1]^n)$) × (factor from $\boldsymbol{A}$). Algebraically, this is *Cauchy's multiplication theorem* (1812).
</details>

**Wo2.** Show that for any invertible $\boldsymbol{A}$, $\det \boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = 1 / \det \boldsymbol{A}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \boldsymbol{I}$, so $\det \boldsymbol{A} \cdot \det \boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = 1$. Thus $\det \boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = 1 / \det \boldsymbol{A}$.
</details>

**Wo3.** The *condition number* of a matrix is $\kappa(\boldsymbol{A}) = |\lambda_{\max}| / |\lambda_{\min}|$ for symmetric positive-definite $\boldsymbol{A}$. What does a large condition number mean for numerical stability of solving $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{b}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Large $\kappa$ means the matrix stretches one direction much more than another. Tiny changes to $\boldsymbol{b}$ (or to the entries of $\boldsymbol{A}$) get amplified by factor $\kappa$ in the solution $\boldsymbol{x}$. **Multicollinearity in regression = large condition number** of $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$ = unstable regression weights. Ridge regression adds $\lambda \boldsymbol{I}$ to floor the smallest eigenvalue, taming $\kappa$ — Lesson 25.
</details>

### Stretch

**S1.** A **Markov chain transition matrix** has rows that sum to 1 (stochastic matrix). Show that $\lambda = 1$ is always an eigenvalue, and find its eigenvector.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at the all-ones column vector $\boldsymbol{1} = (1, 1, \dots, 1)^\top$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For a row-stochastic matrix $\boldsymbol{P}$, $\boldsymbol{P} \boldsymbol{1} = \boldsymbol{1}$ — each row sums to 1, so each entry of $\boldsymbol{P}\boldsymbol{1}$ is the row sum, which is 1. So $\boldsymbol{1}$ is an eigenvector with eigenvalue 1. *The Perron–Frobenius theorem* says this is also the *largest* eigenvalue in magnitude — the stationary distribution lives in the corresponding *left* eigenspace.
</details>

**S2.** The **Hessian** of a smooth function $f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ at a critical point is the matrix $\boldsymbol{H}$ of second partial derivatives. What do the *signs* of its eigenvalues tell you about whether the critical point is a min, max, or saddle?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
All positive: local minimum (Hessian positive definite). All negative: local maximum. Mixed signs: saddle point. Zero eigenvalues: degenerate, need higher-order info. This is the multivariate version of "the second derivative tells you the curvature." We'll use this in Lesson 20 to analyse gradient descent.
</details>

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Determinant = (signed) volume scaling; trace = sum of eigenvalues; eigenvalues = "directions where $\boldsymbol{A}$ acts like a scalar."**
2. **All three are change-of-basis invariants — they belong to the linear map, not to the chosen matrix.**
3. **Symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and an orthonormal eigenbasis — the cleanest case in linear algebra, and the foundation of PCA, SVD, and the Laplacian eigenmaps.**

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Can you explain *why* the determinant equals the product of eigenvalues, to a friend, without notes?
2. Did you solve at least 6 of the 9 practice problems without
   peeking at hints?
3. When you see "eigenvalue $\lambda$" in a paper, do you instantly
   reach for "direction where the matrix is a scalar"?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 11.
- **2 yes:** redo D4 and Wo3, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* problems D5 and D6.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`mml.projections-rotations`](../09-projections-rotations/index.qmd) —
  rotations and projections gave us geometric examples of det = ±1, 0, 1.
- [`t5.eigenvalues`](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd) —
  the pure linear-algebra view of eigenvalues.

### What this unlocks

- [`mml.cholesky`](../11-cholesky/index.qmd) — the first decomposition
  that uses these invariants directly.
- Eventually, [`mml.svd-and-low-rank`](../13-svd-and-low-rank/index.qmd) and
  [`mml.pca-eigenvector-high-dim`](../27-pca-eigenvector-high-dim/index.qmd) —
  every modern decomposition runs on eigenvalues.

### Sister synthesis lesson

[`t5.eigenvalues`](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd) — the synthesis-tier eigenvalue lesson. This one adds ML interpretation: covariance, condition number, Hessian curvature.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Eigenvalues of the covariance matrix = variance in each principal direction (PCA). Eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$ = singular values squared (regression conditioning). Eigenvalues of the Hessian = local curvature (optimisation theory, Newton's method, natural gradients).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** PageRank is the dominant eigenvector of the web's transition matrix. The Google founders' 1998 thesis is essentially "compute that eigenvector." Eigenvalue methods are how spectral clustering finds communities in graphs.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** Many lattice-based cryptosystems have security parameters tied to the smallest singular value (eigenvalue of a hardness matrix). Small eigenvalues → vulnerable; controlled eigenvalues → secure.
:::
